author: @jeffarcher400
text:
Lithium batteries just aren't sustainable. The mining, manufacturing,charging and recycling all use energy. Imagine recycling millions of AA sized batteries.
Is anyone really able to do this?
A liquid electrolyte is simple and more like natural systems.

I've been imagining something I call FREE.
Fast Replaceable Electrolyte Exchange.
A vehicle could pull into the refueling station and the old juice is drained and freshly charged liquid goes back in.
Stations can make their own fuel.
No more exploration, drilling, pumping,refining, storage and transportation.
All countries can be energy independent.
It would require more frequent stops but it's cheap, simple and better for the environment.
This battery seems like the ideal chemistry with a permanent electrolyte and low anode and cathode erosion.

author: @gleambrite2679
text: I truly appreciate all your videos. You do keep things simple and wasy to understand. You are absolutely correct about simplicity. It's also the best teacher. Experiments. Science and development is more than math equations and volumes of chemistry and physics, figuring out complex probabilities and linear graphs ect.. I truly feel science tries to scare people away by making things complicated when it's really not. Many times you don't have to do hours of math trying to figure out every detail. Most of the time each molecule or science has already had all the math and science done on it. Actually, science still doesn't fully understand the ion battery and how sei forms and many other issues.  Actually, they don't understand gravity or the proton for that matter. They didn't understand the nuclear bomb but dropped them anyway. Kudos and full steam ahead. Continue being creative.

author: @19Edurne
text: The longevity may be its downfall. If one battery can last decades without losing its capacity or going bad, it goes against the planned obsolescence mindset of a lot of manufacturers, because you won't need to replace it every four or five years. Alas, most manufacturers are not interested in building things to last nowadays. They 'd rather make good profits than good products. They just want to make things that last just "long enough" – which, for them, means just past the end of warranty.

author: @agritech802
text: Hi Robert, I love your videos and what's more, I love your infectious enthusiasm. This looks like a great battery, quick question, I was researching home battery storage to go along with a potential solar panel installation and prices seemed to be about $1,000 per kWh which was difficult enough to justify considering I'd need 10-15 kWhs of storage but you mentioned that the Bromine battery will cost $50-$60 per kWh. That's a huge difference in price and would be a no brainer if it could be done for even a multiple of that. Am I missing something here, are there any other costs that I'm overlooking or is there really that much of a saving to be made? Thanks a million!

replies.author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
replies.text: I think Robert talks about the pure cost of the batteries in and of itself. Your price tag sounds like it also includes the controlling/loading electronics, installation and cables? And quite possible a hefty and heftier mark-up for a greedy profit margin.

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
Well. As an amateur chemist, I'm glad to see a good use for Bromine.
Thomas Midgely Junior kind of gave me a bad taste for it, when I discovered: He chose, rather arbitrarily and at the last minute, to use chlorofluorocarbons as a refrigerant and aerosol, as opposed to using Bromofluorocarbons. Had he used the latter, the Ozone Layer would have disappeared before we knew there was a problem.

author: @suzannehartmann946
text:
Energy density of this ZnBr battery is 60-80 watt hours per kilo.
Lead acid is 25-40 watt hours per kilo
Lithium is 100-125 watt hours per kilo   (it is explosive just add water) 
Red Flow company is using Zinc Bromide in a flow battery and selling it commercially for cars.  
" The DC to DC round trip efficiency of the ZCell battery is around 80%. This means for every kilowatt-hour of electrical energy put into the battery, only 0.8 kilowatt-hours can be taken out. For a zinc bromine battery this is amazingly high. The Redflow company has clearly done a fantastic job of maximizing the efficiency of their ZCell battery.  Unfortunately, it is still worse than the efficiency of lithium-ion battery systems. For example, the Tesla Powerwall claims to have an efficiency of 92.5% and the LG Chem RESU 95%."

replies.author: @cayrex
replies.text: Lithium ion is in range of 100 to around 265 Wh/kg

replies.author: @abeeconstable7877
replies.text: Actually, that is not entirely correct. The Redflow products are for stationary applications only, due to it being a liquid electrolyte.

replies.author: @sigmasquadleader
replies.text:
Probably not. It would be a titanium alloy, almost certainly.

That may introduce complexities with the reaction that's taking place because of added elements.

I didn't think titanium was that expensive, to be honest. I have a couple samples, including mesh, just because I wanted to have a bunch of different metals on hand for home study chemistry.

Platinum mesh is a bit pricey...

If so, are they any good as an Li ion electrolyte in a water based super capacitor?  Making lithium hydroxide is really easy, just throw some lithium metal into water and that's what it dissolves into!  Great solubility!

author: @totherarf
text:
You make an excellent point about simplicity!

I think we are often bamboozled by complexity and assume the more complex  the better .... I would say the opposite is true!
The real genius ideas are the ones that everyone thinks "Why didn't I think of that? ... Its Obvious"!

The other thing I think is that you were trying to sneak some SteamPunk past us under the radar!
I can just see a few dozen of those beauties linked up to a new solar array to give stored energy on demand to your home!
...... OK maybe in a nice case! ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - these in steampunk cases would be awesome!! - I can actually see how that would look

replies.author: @sigmasquadleader
replies.text:
An engineers job in a perfect world should be to remove every unnecessary design feature without impacting product quality and performance within reason.

There is a famous quote related to this, about perfection being achieved not through the addition of parts but the removal of anything unnecessary. 

Naturally, the real world has redundancies, safety features, intended-use considerations, etc.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @sigmasquadleader ........ and looking Cool! ;o)

author: @kennedy67951
text: I think this is the one Battery I will Build Robert. I really like it. A 100 to 200 KW Home Battery with very thin Clay Plates instead of Pots and I really like the Gel. No spillage. Titanium Screen instead of Zinc Plate. Although Zinc would be easier to source in a pinch, I'm going to look into the Titanium.  What do you think about about this size of Battery Robert? Sure appreciated you sharing your knowledge with me. Thank you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: if you are going to make just one battery then this is it mate - titanium mesh is a good idea and volume will give you energy density and surface area will give you power density it's about 60 Wh/L to give an idea of how much to make

replies.author: @kennedy67951
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks Robert for your opinion and advice. I really think this is the one Battery to Build. I'll have to check out the other Battery tech you have coming soon. P.S Robert what type of Gel would you recommend? Glycol, Agar, Water Glass or Plastic Gels?  Any of these?

author: @endeshaw1000
text: Hello o.o/ I just wanted to say that am glad you make more chemical / battery videos again :). Your videos encourage me a lot, and after was able to produce my own conductive ink (with and without self made graphene) Im making since month nearly every day as lot super condensators as possible, but wew it a nearly endless work XD . My current aim is 400 10x10cm cells. Next time i make bigger ones ;) , but its a better free time activity than simply watching YouTube videos and streams alone. At the moment Im covering the back of the electrodes with synthetic resin varnish, to ensure the electric flow is only between the graphene covered surfaces. Could you tell a trick how to cover "easy" only one side of a steel mesh with synthetic resin varnish? At the moment I cover the opposite side with hair varnish, to prevent the synthetic resin varnish from soaking through, but thats pretty messy and takes so much time -.- . Tape is getting wet and then the surface is extreme bad.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate it is much better to take part than just watch - I suppose you could try creating a  'lake' of the varnish letting it dry then painting it and pressing the mesh into the painted varnish film? - you create the 'lake' on HDPE that way it would peel away easily

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text:
off the top of the head, a hard -paper- roller in a varnish tray.. slowly roll the mesh on that to pick up the varnish without over loading it.
are you applying drying the ink first... do the same there.

replies.author: @endeshaw1000
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering That I will try ! :)

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
Would it be fair to assume you could configure this battery similar to a lead acid design? Anode and cathode close together separated by a porous non-conductive layer, immersed in electrolyte?

In other words would it be feasible to build a zinc brooming battery in a standard lead acid box?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would think so mate - it's zinc bromide by the way - sorry for my accent ! lol

replies.author: @headlessxp4948
replies.text: You could try but the bromine will degrade the plastic and leak out over time. Which isnt that great considering that bromine will, after dissolving skin that has touched it, kill you if you get more than about 1 gram into your body

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering just sweep the south accent under the carpet ,as lease it's not calling power turbines head garment turban's ^_~

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text: As much as I’d like to take credit for coining this a Zink brooming battery we will have to chock this one up to voice to text app clearly working hard to make look even more simple than I am.  Trust me, I do fine without any help.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: It's funny when you think about it, the world spends billions on new technology rather than spending a little to improve the old. The best things in life don't have to be expensive. I wonder how much room it would take to set these up in a sufficient combination of series / parallel cells to replace lead acid or lithium battery packs to run your home solar.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lead is 80-90 Wh/l and ZnBr around 60W/L - so 25% -ish extra space

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering not near as much as I would have guessed. Can this be made into a semi sealed battery to reduce the spillage factor?

author: @wongowonga
text: Very interesting. That's certainly answered some of my question on yesterdays video, thank you. Does anyone know to get hold of bromine in the UK? I see it's sold in tablet/granule form for swimming pools but can't seem to find it in liquid form. I probably being daft, can I just dissolve the tablets in something to get a liquid concentrate? Or should I buy it in liquid form to start with? This chemical stuff is really new to me and so I'm a bit lost as to where I can purchase in the UK. Any advice greatly appreciated.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am glad mate - it was you I had in mind when I talked about cost - why do you want bromine mate? the electrolyte is zinc bromide salt - so what you need is zinc bromide

replies.author: @wongowonga
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Ah, thankyou. I'm clearly getting my wires crossed. Not sure where I got bromine from! I shall search again, at least I'm looking for the correct thing this time! Thanks for all your help Rob, you really are inspiring.

replies.author: @wongowonga
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I have found a supplier! although looking at the handling precautions I think it's best if I learn a bit more about the characteristics before I start messing about! Fairly pricey at £80 for half ltr, but as you say, that would last forever and I would just need to replace the anode and cathode periodically. I turn my hands to many different things, but chemicals has never been one of them! So much to learn but that's what makes it fun. Thanks again Rob.

author: @Earzone63
text:
The brilliant thing about this battery are mentioned before is the fact that Bromine is the only soluble element on the periodic table at room temperature — there are no solid-state crystal structures to get damaged through use over time, which is the same benefit as liquid metal ones, but this one won’t need a smelting furnace so can be made it at home!
I suggested to a friend at pub last night that we make one in an old bathtub he is getting rid of hahaha.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: love that idea mate!

replies.author: @wongowonga
replies.text: Please excuse my ignorance, but wouldn't bathtub of zinc bromide cost an absolute fortune? I just had a quick look at the process for making it but it's way above my paygrade! I just had a look at the price of zinc bromide in the UK and it's nearly £80 for 500grams (which I assume is close to half a ltr?) I'm probably being daft, I'm just trying to learn... chemical are very new to me.

replies.author: @wongowonga
replies.text: I've been thinking about this and realise I have probably missed the point. Are you planning on stripping a metal bath of its enamel and taking advantage of the massive surface area of one of the electrodes? Then partly filling the bath which obviously means not as much ZnBr₂ required? I don't know, just got cogs turning in my head as I try to make sense of this chemical stuff.

replies.author: @Earzone63
replies.text: @wongowonga You were right the first time I just meant to use it as a quirky base container (assuming enamel doesn't corrode ? haha). For my intended purpose of powering a solar house it wouldn't be expensive over the lifetime of its use at all.. it's just a choice to front load the cost. Like the decision to buy a car vs leasing and never owning.

replies.author: @BANANASandDUCTTAPE

author: @aadhithvarathan5432
text: Hi Sir, do you believe Lithium is the solution to fossil fuel problem? Something tells me that while Lithium is a good solution, there is nothing wrong in finding cheaper solutions. Sodium ion batteries maybe?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: rechargeable batteries are never a solution to energy creation mate - the energy to charge them - and indeed make them - must come from somewhere - batteries only store they don't generate

replies.author: @aadhithvarathan5432
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  One of the most fundamental laws of conservation of energy is "Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. It can only be converted from one form to another." Both fossil fuels and lithium ion batteries only convert stored chemical potential energy to electrical or kinetic energy. Today's problems are not based on energy creation, but on energy storage and energy generation. I am very happy that people like you are researching on alternatives to everything. Onboard generation of electricity in an electric car is a very new idea nobody has thought of, and I am sure it will work

replies.author: @dexterdequoitdikkentheworl87
